## Summary

Introduce structured, machine-readable audio analysis. Given a rendered sound (or directory of sounds), extract numeric descriptors: envelope shape, spectral characteristics, loudness, transient count, quality flags.

## User Story

As a developer, I want to analyze generated sounds and get structured metrics (duration, peak, RMS, spectral centroid, quality flags) so I can objectively compare outputs and catch quality issues automatically.

## What the User Sees

```bash
toneforge generate --recipe weapon-laser-zap --seed-range 1:20 --output ./renders/
toneforge analyze --input ./renders/ --output ./analysis/
toneforge analyze --input ./renders/weapon-laser-zap_seed-001.wav --format json
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge analyze --input <path> --output <dir> [--format json|table]`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Analysis extracts at minimum: duration, peak, RMS, crest factor, attack time, spectral centroid
2. Quality flags detect clipping (peak > 1.0) and silence (RMS < threshold)
3. Batch analysis produces one JSON file per input
4. Table format outputs a human-readable summary
5. Analysis results are deterministic for the same input file

## Reference PRDs

- `ANALYZE_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 4)

## Dependencies

- Demo 2: WAV Export & Sample-Hybrid Recipes (prerequisite)
data.title: Demo 4: Audio Analysis -- Measuring What You Made

data.description:
## Summary

Add semantic labeling to analyzed sounds. Given analysis data, assign meaningful categories, materials, intensity levels, texture descriptors, and use-case tags.

## User Story

As a developer, I want generated sounds to be automatically classified with semantic labels (category, material, intensity, texture, tags) so I can search and filter sounds by meaning rather than filenames.

## What the User Sees

```bash
toneforge classify --analysis ./analysis/ --output ./classification/
toneforge classify --input ./renders/weapon-laser-zap_seed-001.wav --format json
toneforge classify search --category weapon --intensity high --texture sharp
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge classify --analysis <dir> --output <dir>`
- `toneforge classify --input <path>` (end-to-end: analyze then classify)
- `toneforge classify search --category <c> [--intensity <i>] [--texture <t>]`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Classification assigns at minimum: category, intensity, texture, tags
2. Rule-based pre-classifier produces reasonable labels for known recipe types
3. Search returns matching entries from classification data
4. Classification output is deterministic for the same analysis input

## Reference PRDs

- `CLASSIFY_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 5)

## Dependencies

- Demo 4: Audio Analysis (prerequisite)
data.title: Demo 5: Classification -- Semantic Labels for Sounds

data.description:
## Summary

Demonstrate systematic discovery across large seed/parameter spaces. Generate thousands of variations, rank them by analysis metrics, cluster them by similarity, and surface the most interesting or useful sounds.

## User Story

As a developer, I want to explore thousands of seed variations and automatically find the best-sounding results ranked by objective metrics so I can discover sounds I would never find manually.

## What the User Sees

```bash
toneforge explore --recipe weapon-laser-zap --seed-range 0:10000 --keep-top 50 --rank-by transient-density --output ./exploration/
toneforge explore mutate --seed 4821 --recipe weapon-laser-zap --jitter 0.1 --count 20 --output ./mutations/
toneforge explore promote --seed 4821 --recipe weapon-laser-zap
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge explore --recipe <r> --seed-range <s:e> --keep-top <n> --rank-by <metric>`
- `toneforge explore mutate --seed <n> --recipe <r> --jitter <f> --count <n>`
- `toneforge explore promote --seed <n> --recipe <r>`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Seed sweep of 10,000+ completes in under 2 minutes
2. Ranking uses at least 3 selectable metrics (transient-density, spectral-centroid, RMS)
3. Clustering groups results into 3-8 clusters
4. Mutation produces variations perceptibly similar to but distinct from the source
5. `promote` marks a result for library import

## Reference PRDs

- `EXPLORE_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 6)

## Dependencies

- Demo 4: Audio Analysis (prerequisite)
- Demo 5: Classification (prerequisite)

## Summary

Introduce persistent, indexed storage for curated sounds. The library stores audio files, presets, analysis, and classification data in a unified, queryable structure with deterministic regeneration support.

## User Story

As a developer, I want a persistent library that stores curated sounds with full metadata, supports search by attributes, and can deterministically regenerate any sound from its stored preset.

## What the User Sees

```bash
toneforge library import --from ./exploration/ --category weapon
toneforge library list --category weapon
toneforge library search --intensity high --texture sharp
toneforge library similar --id weapon/laser-zap/seed-4821 --limit 5
toneforge library export --category weapon --format wav --output ./export/
toneforge library regenerate --id weapon/laser-zap/seed-4821
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge library import --from <dir> --category <c>`
- `toneforge library list [--category <c>]`
- `toneforge library search [--intensity <i>] [--texture <t>] [--tags <t>]`
- `toneforge library similar --id <id> --limit <n>`
- `toneforge library export --category <c> --format wav --output <dir>`
- `toneforge library regenerate --id <id>`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Library stores metadata, analysis, classification, and preset alongside audio
2. Search returns relevant results across multiple attribute dimensions
3. Similarity search finds perceptually related sounds
4. Regeneration from stored presets produces byte-identical output
5. Export produces valid WAV files organized by category

## Reference PRDs

- `LIBRARY_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 7)

## Dependencies

- Demo 6: Exploration (prerequisite)

## Summary

Move from single sound events to temporal patterns. The Sequencer schedules when events occur over time -- footstep cadences, weapon fire bursts, ambient soundscapes. This is the 'when' axis of ToneForge's What/When/Why model.

## User Story

As a game developer, I want to define temporal sound patterns (footstep cadences, weapon bursts, ambient loops) as JSON presets so I can create realistic sound behaviors that vary per-event while maintaining consistent timing.

## What the User Sees

```bash
toneforge sequence generate --preset presets/sequences/footsteps_walk.json --duration 5s --seed 42 --output ./walk_sequence.wav
toneforge sequence generate --preset presets/sequences/footsteps_run.json --duration 5s --seed 42 --output ./run_sequence.wav
toneforge sequence simulate --preset presets/sequences/footsteps_walk.json --duration 5s
toneforge sequence inspect --preset presets/sequences/footsteps_walk.json
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge sequence generate --preset <file> --duration <t> --seed <n> --output <path>`
- `toneforge sequence simulate --preset <file> --duration <t>`
- `toneforge sequence inspect --preset <file>`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. At least 3 sequence presets exist (footsteps_walk, footsteps_run, weapon_burst)
2. Sequences produce correctly timed events with per-event variation
3. `simulate` outputs an accurate event timeline
4. Sequence rendering is deterministic
5. Duration flag controls total output length

## Reference PRDs

- `SEQUENCER_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 8)

## Dependencies

- Demo 3: Sound Stacking (prerequisite)
- Demo 7: Library (prerequisite)

data.description:
## Summary

Demonstrate real-time playback with state-driven behavior changes. A character transitions from walking to running to sprinting, and the sound behavior (cadence, intensity, recipe selection) changes in response. Environmental context (surface material) modifies the sound.

## User Story

As a game developer, I want sound behavior to respond to game state (walk/run/sprint) and environmental context (stone/gravel surface) in real time so that audio feels alive and responsive rather than static.

## What the User Sees

Interactive session (web demo or REPL):
```
> runtime.start()
> context.set({ surface: "stone" })
> state.set("movement", "walk")
  [Playing: footsteps_walk on stone, cadence=0.6s, intensity=0.7]
> state.set("movement", "run")
  [Transition: walk -> run]
> context.set({ surface: "gravel" })
  [Context changed: surface stone -> gravel]
> state.inspect()
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge runtime start` (interactive mode)
- State and context manipulation via REPL or API

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Runtime plays sequences in real time with audible output
2. State changes trigger sequence transitions (walk -> run -> sprint)
3. Context changes modify recipe selection (stone -> gravel footstep recipe)
4. State inspection shows current values, history, and active sequences
5. All transitions are logged and deterministically reproducible given the same event sequence

## Reference PRDs

- `RUNTIME_PRD.md`, `STATE_PRD.md`, `CONTEXT_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 9)

## Dependencies

- Demo 8: Sequencer (prerequisite)
data.title: Demo 9: Runtime & State -- Real-Time Behavioral Sound

data.description:
## Summary

Demonstrate behavior-aware mixing under load. Multiple concurrent sound sources (footsteps + combat + UI + ambient) are dynamically balanced based on game state, preventing audio chaos.

## User Story

As a game developer, I want a mixer that automatically ducks ambient sounds during combat, prioritizes UI events, and limits concurrent voices so that audio never becomes an uncontrolled wall of noise.

## What the User Sees

Interactive session continuing from Demo 9:
```
> state.set("movement", "walk")
> sequence.start("ambient_wind")
> sequence.start("combat_distant")
> mixer.inspect()
  Mix Groups:
    footsteps    [-6dB]  priority=medium  voices=1/4
    ambience     [-12dB] priority=low     voices=1/2
    combat       [-8dB]  priority=high    voices=1/4
> state.set("situation", "combat_active")
  [Mixer: ambience ducked -6dB, combat boosted +3dB]
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Mix groups with configurable gain, priority, and voice limits
2. State-driven ducking/boosting rules applied automatically
3. UI events trigger brief footstep ducking
4. Voice limiting prevents exceeding max concurrent sources
5. `mixer.inspect()` displays current state of all groups

## Reference PRDs

- `MIXER_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 10)

## Dependencies

- Demo 9: Runtime & State (prerequisite)

## Summary

Demonstrate the reasoning layer that synthesizes analysis, classification, and library data to provide actionable recommendations. Intelligence is assistive -- it suggests, the human decides.

## User Story

As a developer managing a sound library, I want an intelligent assistant that audits my library for gaps and redundancy, recommends sounds for specific use cases, and suggests exploration targets so I can build a complete, high-quality sound collection efficiently.

## What the User Sees

```bash
toneforge intelligence audit --library ./library
toneforge intelligence recommend --use-case "sci-fi menu navigation" --max-results 5
toneforge intelligence suggest-exploration --recipe footstep-stone
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge intelligence audit --library <dir>`
- `toneforge intelligence recommend --use-case <desc> --max-results <n>`
- `toneforge intelligence suggest-exploration --recipe <r>`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Audit identifies coverage gaps, redundancy, and quality issues
2. Recommendations include confidence scores and explanations
3. Exploration suggestions reference specific recipes and seed ranges
4. No Intelligence action modifies library data without human approval
5. All suggestions reference actionable CLI commands

## Reference PRDs

- `INTELLIGENCE_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 11)

## Dependencies

- Demo 7: Library (prerequisite)
- Demo 10: Mixer (prerequisite)
data.title: Demo 11: Intelligence -- Assisted Decision-Making

data.description:
## Summary

Transform curated library content into platform-specific, production-ready artifacts. The Validator ensures quality standards before compilation. The Compiler decides what stays procedural vs. what gets baked to WAV, and packages everything for target platforms.

## User Story

As a developer preparing for release, I want to validate my sound library against platform-specific quality rules and compile it into optimized, production-ready packages with deterministic build manifests.

## What the User Sees

```bash
toneforge validate --library ./library --ruleset mobile --strictness warning
toneforge compile --library ./library --category weapon --target web --rules presets/compile/web_defaults.json --output ./dist/web/
toneforge compile --library ./library --target mobile --rules presets/compile/mobile_aggressive.json --output ./dist/mobile/
toneforge compile --library ./library --target web --dry-run
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge validate --library <dir> --ruleset <name> --strictness <level>`
- `toneforge compile --library <dir> --category <c> --target <platform> --rules <file> --output <dir>`
- `toneforge compile --dry-run`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Validator checks: peak clipping, duration bounds, silence ratio
2. Validator produces structured JSON report with pass/warning/error counts
3. Compiler produces WAV files + manifest.json for target platform
4. Dry-run shows decisions without writing files
5. Build hashes enable deterministic build verification

## Reference PRDs

- `COMPILER_PRD.md`, `VALIDATOR_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 12)

## Dependencies

- Demo 7: Library (prerequisite)
- Demo 11: Intelligence (prerequisite)

## Summary

Extend ToneForge beyond audio. The Visualizer generates deterministic, audio-synchronized visual effects. Haptics produces tactile feedback patterns. Palettes coordinate the aesthetic across all outputs.

## User Story

As a game developer, I want ToneForge to generate synchronized visual effects and haptic feedback patterns alongside audio so that all sensory outputs are perceptually aligned and aesthetically coordinated through palettes.

## What the User Sees

Web demo with visual canvas:
```
> palette.set("sci_fi_neon")
> runtime.play("weapon-laser-zap", { seed: 42, visual: true, haptic: true })
  [Audio: laser zap plays]
  [Visual: cyan directional streak, 150ms]
  [Haptic: sharp pulse, 50ms, intensity=0.8]
> palette.set("organic_forest")
> runtime.play("footstep-stone", { seed: 42, visual: true, haptic: true })
  [Visual: subtle ground ripple, earth tones]
  [Haptic: soft thump, 30ms, intensity=0.4]
```

```bash
toneforge visualize export --recipe weapon-laser-zap --seed 42 --format spritesheet --frames 8 --output ./vfx/
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge visualize export --recipe <r> --seed <n> --format <f> --output <dir>`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Visualizer produces animated effects synchronized to audio events
2. Haptic patterns match audio intensity and timing
3. Palette changes visually and perceptually alter all outputs
4. Sprite sheet export produces usable game assets
5. All outputs are deterministic for the same seed and palette

## Summary

Demonstrate structured human goal representation and long-term experiential recall. Intent translates high-level directives ("make combat punchier") into actionable Intelligence queries. Memory tracks how assets are used, evaluated, and evolved over time.

## Prerequisite Demos

- Demo 11: Intelligence -- Assisted Decision-Making (TF-0MLXF98WZ1LQGM5W)

## New Modules

Intent, Memory

## What the User Sees

- Submit a structured intent: `toneforge intent submit --goal "reduce repetition in footstep sounds" --scope footsteps`
- Intelligence analysis produces actionable suggestions with human approval gate
- Query memory for historical context: `toneforge memory query --scope "weapon sounds" --time-range "last 30 days"`
- Memory informs intelligence recommendations via `--use-memory` flag

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge intent submit --goal <desc> --scope <scope>`
- `toneforge memory query --scope <desc> --time-range <range>`
- `--use-memory` flag on intelligence commands

## Key Proof Points

- High-level goals translate into concrete, executable actions
- Memory prevents repeating mistakes and surfaces patterns humans miss
- Intent -> Intelligence pipeline maintains human approval at every step
- Historical data improves recommendation quality over time

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Intent submission produces an Intelligence analysis with actionable suggestions
2. No action executes without human approval
3. Memory records generation, promotion, and rejection events
4. Memory queries return time-scoped, scope-filtered reports
5. Intelligence recommendations improve when memory data is available

## Reference PRDs

INTENT_PRD.md, MEMORY_PRD.md
data.title: Demo 14: Intent & Memory -- Human-Centered Guidance

data.description:
## Summary

Demonstrate ToneForge operating across multiple clients in sync and embedded within external tools. Network replicates behavioral events (not audio streams) for deterministic multi-client playback. Integrations show ToneForge driving sound in a CI pipeline and exporting to game engine formats.

## Prerequisite Demos

- Demo 9: Runtime & State -- Real-Time Behavioral Sound (TF-0MLXF8TBT0DJDCO1)
- Demo 12: Compiler & Validator -- Production-Ready Assets (TF-0MLXF9FWG18T2ZHD)

## New Modules

Network, Integrations

## What the User Sees

**Network (two browser windows side by side):**
- Host broadcasts state changes to connected peers
- Client receives state changes and plays deterministically identical audio
- State transitions are synchronized across all connected clients

**Integrations (CI pipeline):**
- Generate, validate, compile, and export sounds in automated pipelines
- Export for Unity/web with platform-appropriate file structure

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge sync --target <engine> --library <path> --output <path>`
- Network API: `network.host()`, `network.join()`

## Key Proof Points

- Network sync uses behavioral events (~50 bytes each), not audio streams (~1 MB/s)
- Determinism guarantees identical playback on all clients from the same events
- CI integration enables automated sound generation, validation, and packaging
- Engine adapters map ToneForge categories to platform-specific audio groups

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Two clients play the same sound sequence from shared behavioral events
2. Network bandwidth is under 1 KB/s for typical gameplay
3. CI pipeline validates, compiles, and exports without manual intervention
4. Unity/web export produces platform-appropriate file structure
5. Late-join clients receive state snapshot and sync correctly

## Reference PRDs

NETWORK_PRD.md, INTEGRATIONS_PRD.md

## Summary

Demonstrate publishing and consuming ToneForge assets through a marketplace. Assets are procedural systems (recipes, stacks, sequences, palettes), not static audio files -- buyers get infinite variation from a single purchase.

## Prerequisite Demos

- Demo 12: Compiler & Validator -- Production-Ready Assets (TF-0MLXF9FWG18T2ZHD)
- Demo 15: Network & Integrations -- Distributed & Embedded (TF-0MLXFBHHW1BBO1ZJ)

## New Modules

Marketplace

## What the User Sees

- Browse marketplace listings: `toneforge marketplace search --category "sci-fi weapons"`
- Install packages: `toneforge marketplace install industrial_lasers@2.1.0`
- Use installed recipes immediately with local registry
- Publish own packages with validation: `toneforge marketplace publish --package ./my-recipes/ --name "organic_footsteps" --version 1.0.0`

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge marketplace search --category <c>`
- `toneforge marketplace install <package>@<version>`
- `toneforge marketplace publish --package <dir> --name <n> --version <v>`

## Key Proof Points

- Marketplace assets are procedural systems, not static files -- infinite variation from a single package
- Validator gates publishing -- no broken or invalid assets reach the marketplace
- Semantic versioning ensures compatibility
- Installed recipes integrate seamlessly with the local registry

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Search returns relevant packages with metadata (recipes, stacks, ratings, license)
2. Install adds recipes to the local registry and they work immediately
3. Publish validates the package before uploading
4. Installed packages maintain determinism (same seed = same output)
5. Version conflicts are detected and reported

## Reference PRDs

MARKETPLACE_PRD.md

## Summary

Author `demos/mvp-1.md` by extracting narrative content from the bash script and TypeScript data file, and document the markdown convention in `demos/README.md`.

## Player Experience Change

No direct user-visible change. This feature creates the source-of-truth content file that all subsequent features consume. Demo authors and reviewers can now read and edit demo content in standard markdown.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `demos/mvp-1.md` contains all narrative content from `scripts/demo/mvp_1.sh` and `web/src/demo-content.ts` (intro, 4 acts, recap)

## Summary

The intro and recap steps in both the CLI demo runner and web wizard display their text content as a wall of text with no visual separation between paragraphs and lists. The act steps (which use problem/solution/commands/commentary) look great.

## Root Cause

The parser's nodesToDescription function joins paragraphs with single newlines, collapsing distinct paragraphs into a continuous block. Both renderers then display this flat text without structural formatting:
- CLI: printDescription prints line-by-line with no blank line between paragraphs
- Web: puts the entire description into a `<pre>` tag, showing it as monospace with no paragraph breaks

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Parser separates paragraphs with double newlines (\n\n) in the description field
2. CLI runner renders description text with proper blank lines between paragraphs and visual distinction for list items
3. Web wizard renders description as structured HTML (paragraphs and ordered/unordered lists) instead of a `<pre>` block
4. All existing tests pass (root 98, web 35, E2E 5)
5. Build succeeds

## Summary

Create `bin/dev-cli.js` that dynamically registers tsx and imports `src/cli.ts`, and update `package.json` `bin` field to register both `tf` and `toneforge`.

## Context

Part of Install the CLI (TF-0MLW2K2V51AY2N9T). No `bin/` directory exists. The current `package.json` maps `"toneforge": "dist/cli.js"`. This feature creates the dev-mode loader that allows running the CLI from TypeScript source without a build step.

### User Experience Change

Developers can run `./bin/dev-cli.js generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42` directly, getting the same result as `npx tsx src/cli.ts generate ...` but with a shorter, more conventional path. Code changes in `src/` are reflected immediately without rebuilding.

### Reference Documents

- `docs/prd/CLI_PRD.md` -- CLI command model
- TF-0MLW3L9SN041842J -- CLI startup performance (tsx overhead findings)
- `src/cli.ts` -- CLI entry point that the loader imports

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `bin/dev-cli.js` exists with `#!/usr/bin/env node` shebang, dynamically imports tsx to register TypeScript support, then imports `src/cli.ts` and calls `main()`
2. `package.json` `bin` field maps both `tf` and `toneforge` to `bin/dev-cli.js`
3. `./bin/dev-cli.js generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42` produces the same output as `npx tsx src/cli.ts generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42`
4. After editing a console.log string in `src/cli.ts`, running `./bin/dev-cli.js --help` reflects the change without running `npm run build`

6. `bin/dev-cli.js` has the executable permission bit set
7. Startup time for `tf generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42` is under 5 seconds

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `bin/dev-cli.js` with shebang, tsx registration via `await import('tsx/esm/api')`, then `const { main } = await import('../src/cli.ts'); process.exitCode = await main();`
- Update `package.json` `bin` to `{ "tf": "bin/dev-cli.js", "toneforge": "bin/dev-cli.js" }`

None (first feature in the chain)

- Updated `package.json`

## Summary

Refactor the offline renderer to discover recipes via the RecipeRegistry instead of hardcoded `SUPPORTED_RECIPES` array and per-recipe switch statements, extending the recipe registration to include `getParams`, `getDuration`, and `buildOfflineGraph` capabilities.

## Context

Part of Demo 1: Recipe Variety (TF-0MLXF74KE17SQKAD). The current renderer (`src/core/renderer.ts`) maintains a hardcoded `SUPPORTED_RECIPES` array and uses per-recipe switch statements in `buildRecipeGraph()` and `getRecipeDuration()`. This makes adding new recipes cumbersome — every new recipe requires modifying the renderer. This feature refactors the renderer to use the RecipeRegistry for discovery, and extends the registration entry to include params, duration, and offline graph builder capabilities.

### User Experience Change

No direct user-facing change. After this feature, adding a new recipe no longer requires modifying `renderer.ts` or `cli.ts` — only registering in `src/recipes/index.ts`. This unblocks all subsequent recipe features.

### Reference Documents

- `docs/prd/CORE_PRD.md` Section 4.1 (Recipe interface), Section 8 (offline rendering)
- `src/core/renderer.ts` — current hardcoded renderer
- `src/core/recipe.ts` — RecipeRegistry class
- `src/recipes/index.ts` — recipe registration

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `SUPPORTED_RECIPES` constant and `buildRecipeGraph`/`getRecipeDuration` switch statements are removed from `renderer.ts`
2. The renderer resolves recipe name to an offline graph builder via the RecipeRegistry
3. Adding a new recipe requires only: (a) creating param + factory files, (b) registering in `src/recipes/index.ts`. No changes to `renderer.ts` or `cli.ts` are needed
4. Existing `ui-scifi-confirm` renderer determinism tests (10x byte-identical) continue to pass unchanged
5. The Recipe registration entry includes `getParams(rng)`, `getDuration(rng)`, and `buildOfflineGraph(rng, ctx, duration)` callables
6. `ui-scifi-confirm` is migrated to the new registration shape
7. `cli.ts` uses the registry for recipe validation instead of `SUPPORTED_RECIPES`

## Minimal Implementation

- Extend the `RecipeRegistry.register()` signature to accept params/duration/offlineBuilder alongside the Tone.js factory
- Migrate `ui-scifi-confirm` registration in `src/recipes/index.ts` to the new shape
- Refactor `renderRecipe()` in `renderer.ts` to look up the recipe from the registry and call its offline builder
- Remove `SUPPORTED_RECIPES`, `buildRecipeGraph`, `getRecipeDuration`
- Update `src/cli.ts` to use the registry for recipe validation
- Run all existing tests to confirm no regressions

## Dependencies

None (first feature in the chain)

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/core/renderer.ts`
- Updated `src/core/recipe.ts`
- Updated `src/recipes/index.ts`
- Updated `src/cli.ts`
- All existing tests green

## Summary

## Context

Part of Demo 1: Recipe Variety (TF-0MLXF74KE17SQKAD). This is one of 4 new recipes demonstrating that ToneForge's recipe architecture generalizes across game audio categories. The weapon category is typically well-suited to pure synthesis, making this a lower-risk recipe.

### User Experience Change

Developers can run `toneforge generate --recipe weapon-laser-zap --seed 42` to hear a procedurally generated laser/blaster sound. Different seeds produce distinct variations in pitch, modulation depth, and noise burst character.

### Reference Documents

## Context

Part of Demo 1: Recipe Variety (TF-0MLXF74KE17SQKAD). This recipe demonstrates that the procedural recipe pattern works for impact/transient sounds. The epic notes that footstep sounds are harder to synthesize convincingly with pure synthesis than UI sounds; the goal is architectural validation, not sonic perfection.

### User Experience Change

Developers can run `toneforge generate --recipe footstep-stone --seed 42` to hear a procedurally generated footstep sound on a hard surface. Different seeds produce distinct variations in transient character, resonance, and tonal quality.

### Reference Documents

## Summary

Add a `toneforge list recipes` subcommand that outputs all registered recipe names from the RecipeRegistry.

## Context

Part of Demo 1: Recipe Variety (TF-0MLXF74KE17SQKAD). The CLI currently only supports the `generate` command. Adding `list recipes` enables developers to discover all available recipes without reading source code, which is a key user story in the epic.

### User Experience Change

Developers can run `toneforge list recipes` to see all registered recipe names. This replaces the need to read source code or documentation to discover available recipes.

### Reference Documents

- `docs/prd/CLI_PRD.md` Section 4.1, 5.1 — verb-first CLI command model
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` line 76 — `toneforge list recipes` command
- `src/cli.ts` — current CLI entry point

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `toneforge list recipes` outputs all registered recipe names, one per line
2. Exit code 0 on success
3. `toneforge list --help` prints usage information for the list command
4. `toneforge list` without a subresource prints an error with usage hint
5. `toneforge list invalid-thing` prints an error with usage hint and exits with code 1
6. Help text for `toneforge --help` includes `list` in the commands section
7. CLI tests cover the new command (unit + integration)

## Summary

## Context

The `isGenerateCommand()` function only matches commands containing `--recipe ui-scifi-confirm`. This feature makes the web demo generic so it works with any registered recipe.

Developers visiting the web demo can select any registered recipe from a dropdown, generate sounds with different seeds, and hear them in the browser. The recipe-variety demo walkthrough is available alongside the MVP demo in the wizard selector.

## Summary

Create a new markdown-driven demo walkthrough (`demos/recipe-variety.md`) showcasing recipe variety across categories, seed variation within a category, and the `toneforge list recipes` command.

## Context

Part of Demo 1: Recipe Variety (TF-0MLXF74KE17SQKAD). The existing demo engine (`demos/`) supports markdown-driven walkthroughs following the convention in `demos/README.md`. This feature creates the Demo 1 walkthrough that showcases the new recipes and `list` command.

### User Experience Change

Developers can follow a guided demo walkthrough that showcases ToneForge's recipe variety: discovering recipes, generating sounds across different categories, hearing seed variation within a single recipe, and comparing sounds across categories. Available in both the CLI demo runner and the web wizard.

### Reference Documents

- `demos/README.md` — demo markdown convention (front matter, heading structure, content semantics)
- `demos/mvp-1.md` — reference demo following the convention
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` lines 47-93 — Demo 1 specification
- `src/demo/parser.ts` — demo markdown parser

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `demos/recipe-variety.md` exists following the demo markdown convention (YAML front matter with title/id/description, `## ` step headings, blockquotes for problems, code blocks for commands, admonitions for commentary)
2. Demo covers: discovering recipes (`list`), generating from multiple categories, seed variation within a single recipe, and cross-category comparison
3. All bash code blocks reference valid CLI commands that work with the implemented recipes
4. Demo parses correctly via the existing demo parser (validated by parser tests)
5. Narrative follows the problem/solution pattern established by `mvp-1.md`

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `demos/recipe-variety.md` with 4-6 steps:
  1. Intro: the recipe variety problem (only one recipe type is not enough)
  2. Discover: `toneforge list recipes` to see all available recipes
  3. Generate across categories: weapon-laser-zap, footstep-stone, ui-notification-chime, ambient-wind-gust
  4. Seed variation: same recipe with different seeds to hear variation within a category
  5. Cross-category comparison: side-by-side generation of different recipe types
  6. Recap: what was demonstrated
- Follow the problem > solution > command > commentary pattern from mvp-1.md
- Run parser tests to validate markdown format
- Add parser integration test for the new demo file

- Feature 7: CLI List Recipes Command (TF-0MLYAN81319GEVJ8)

|---|-----|-------|-------------|

## Key Decisions

- Markdown parser: remark (unified/mdast)
- Vite import: resolve alias in vite.config.ts
- Multi-demo UI: interactive CLI list picker + styled web dropdown
- Interface shape: parser-first, wizard/CLI adapt
- Rollout: branch-based cutover, no feature flags
- E2E tests: in-scope
- Convention docs: demos/README.md
- Parser tests: golden snapshot + edge case tests

## Dependency Graph

```
Feature 1 (Content)
  └── Feature 2 (Parser)
        ├── Feature 3 (CLI Runner)
        └── Feature 4 (Web Adapter)
              └── Feature 5 (Tests)
                    └── Feature 6 (Cleanup)
```

Features 3 and 4 can be worked in parallel after Feature 2 is complete.

## Open Questions

None -- all questions resolved during interview.

data.author: opencode
data.comment:
Completed implementation and all tests pass (62 root + 35 web unit + 5 Playwright E2E = 102 total). Commit 1392674 pushed to main.

Changes:
- web/src/audio.ts: Converted static import * as Tone from tone to dynamic import() via loadAudioDeps(). Pure functions (extractSeed, isGenerateCommand) remain synchronous. Tone.js only loads on user click.
- web/src/terminal.ts: Replaced fixed 3s reconnect with exponential backoff (1s initial, 15s max, 10 max attempts). Silent retries before first connection. Helpful Backend not available message after max retries.
- web/vite.config.ts: Added changeOrigin: true to /ws proxy config.
- web/test/audio.test.ts: Removed vi.mock(tone) and dynamic await import() since audio.ts no longer imports tone at top level. Direct static import of pure functions.

All 6 acceptance criteria satisfied.

Changes:
- web/src/terminal.ts: Added console.log/warn/error for all WebSocket lifecycle events. Shows Connecting to backend... immediately so the terminal is never blank. Logs sendCommand with command text. Warns in console when Run fails due to disconnected WebSocket.
- web/e2e/tutorial.spec.ts: Updated tests to verify browser console contains [ToneForge] WebSocket connected log, zero AudioContext errors, and 6 Sending command messages during full walkthrough.

This addresses the silent failure issue where the terminal panel showed nothing and the browser console had no relevant logs.

data.author: opencode
data.comment:
Added dev:web convenience script. Commit 1a0326e pushed to main.

npm run dev:web from the project root now:
1. Builds the root project (tsc)
2. Builds the web project (vite build + tsc server)
3. Starts Express backend on port 3000 and Vite dev server on port 5173 concurrently
4. Ctrl+C kills both processes cleanly

Uses concurrently with color-coded labels: [backend] (blue) and [vite] (green).

Fixed intro/recap formatting in both CLI and web demo. Three changes: 1. **Parser** (src/demo/parser.ts): nodesToDescription now separates paragraphs and list blocks with double newlines instead of single newlines, preserving paragraph structure. 2. **Web wizard** (web/src/wizard.ts): Description rendering changed from a single `
` tag to structured HTML -- paragraphs become `
` elements, ordered lists become `
    `/`
  1. `, unordered lists become `
      `/`
    • `.

3. **Web CSS** (web/src/index.html): Replaced `.wizard-description pre` styles with styles for `p`, `ol`, `ul`, and `li` elements with proper margins and padding.

CLI runner needed no changes -- it already splits on newlines, and the double newlines naturally produce blank lines between paragraphs.
